headlines.geopolitics: Navy veteran given full military honors as community refuses to let him be buried alone
headlines.economy: Deputy AG announces new DOJ fraud division to combat 'insane' government benefit fraud
headlines.domestic: Senate moves toward passing sweeping housing bill ahead of midterms

metadata.daily_do: Check if there are any unclaimed veteran burials in your area that need community support

clips.caption: When Duke gets unexpectedly emotional about veteran burials
Pringle explains why we honor unknown soldiers
Jax discovers the wildest workplace harassment case ever

script.text: I looked at Jenny this morning and said honey, if I die first and nobody shows up to my funeral, just bury me in the backyard next to the grill.
It's Tuesday, March 11th, and this is The Friction. I'm Leo Barros with Dr. Haywood Pringle, Bree Aldana, Duke Patel, and Jax Mooney. Three headlines to start us off.

Geopolitics first. Navy veteran Lonnie Wayman was buried yesterday at Middle Tennessee State Veterans Cemetery. No family came forward to claim his remains, so the community did. Hundreds of people showed up to give him full military honors.
Good.
That's beautiful.
The VA rep said quote, when the paperwork came across my desk marked unclaimed veteran, I say that's incorrect. Thanks to community support, we are able to claim our honorable veterans.
I'm not crying, you're crying.

Economy. Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche announced a new DOJ fraud division yesterday to combat what he calls insane levels of government benefit fraud. He cited cases including two Pennsylvania men who fraudulently received four million dollars for fake Minneapolis homeless shelters.
Four million? Jesus.
Wait, fake shelters? Like they didn't exist at all?
Correct. Blanche said the federal government gives money away like candy, citing widespread school lunch and daycare fraud. Quote, turns out it's mostly made up, it's crazy.
Mostly made up? That's like saying I'm mostly employed.

And domestic. The Senate is moving toward passing comprehensive housing legislation that could address affordability issues expected to dominate the midterm elections. The bill's progress is considered surprising given how politically charged housing costs have become.
Finally. I've been stress-eating chickpeas about housing policy since like, 2019.
Sweeping legislation. My favorite kind. Nothing could go wrong there.
We'll dig into the fraud story in a moment, but first let's talk about Lonnie Wayman. Because honestly, this feels like the kind of story we need more of.

So here's what happened. Navy veteran, no known family, remains go unclaimed. Standard procedure would be a basic burial. Instead, the community rallied. Veterans groups, random citizens, military personnel. Hundreds of people.
I mean, this is what community looks like, right? Like, actual community. Not the word we throw around on social media.
You know what gets me? The VA guy saying the paperwork was wrong. That it wasn't really unclaimed because the community claimed him.
That's actually profound. Like, we're redefining what family means in real time.
The bigger question though, should this be happening? Should we rely on community goodwill, or should the government be doing more to track veteran families in the first place?
I mean, both? Like, the VA should absolutely have better systems for— wait, do we know how many veterans this happens to annually?
The numbers aren't tracked consistently across states. That's part of the problem.
Look, I'm all for better systems, but here's the thing. This community response? It's not a bug, it's a feature. It's how it should work.
But Duke, what about veterans in communities that don't have the resources or the organizational capacity to—
Then we build that capacity. You don't solve community problems by replacing community with bureaucracy.
Okay but that's— that's literally what I'm saying. We need systems AND community. The VA should be connecting with local veteran groups, not just sending paperwork.
Can I just say, the image of hundreds of strangers showing up to honor someone they never met? That's the America I want to live in.
Exactly. And you know what? I bet half those people drove an hour to get there. On a Tuesday morning. That means something.
The ceremony ended with the release of doves. The priest said eternal rest grant unto him, O Lord, and let perpetual light shine upon him.
Oh man. Now I'm actually tearing up.
You know what I told Jenny this morning? I said honey, if I die first and nobody shows up to my funeral, just bury me in the backyard next to the grill.
Duke, that's the most Tennessee thing you've ever said and you live in Tennessee.
I'm serious though. That grill has fed more people than most restaurants.
Okay but the real question is, how do we make sure this happens for every veteran who needs it? Because Nashville has a strong veteran community. What about rural areas?
That's where federal coordination could actually help. Not replacing community effort, but amplifying it.
I can live with that. As long as the feds don't mess it up by turning it into a program with forms and waiting periods.
Right, it should be like, the VA reaches out to local American Legion posts, VFW chapters. Connect the dots, don't control the process.
This is like the one government program I could actually get behind. Operation No Veteran Dies Alone.
Alright, let's shift gears to something that'll make us all significantly angrier. Government fraud.
Finally, a topic where Bree and I can agree.
Wait, don't get ahead of yourself. I'm sure we'll find something to fight about.
Deputy AG Todd Blanche says fraud is insane levels. Two guys from Pennsylvania, never lived in Minneapolis, got four million dollars for homeless shelters that didn't exist.
I'm sorry, four million? For shelters that don't exist? I can barely get my landlord to fix my actual shower.
This is exactly why I don't trust big government programs. You throw money at problems without basic oversight and this is what happens.
Okay but Duke, the problem isn't the programs themselves, it's the oversight. We need better verification systems, not no programs.
Bree, they literally didn't check if the shelters existed. That's not oversight, that's basic competence.
Which is why I'm saying we need better systems! You can't just— look, homeless people still need services. The answer isn't to stop funding homeless services.
Blanche also mentioned school lunch fraud and fake daycare centers. Quote, the feds get a bill saying hey, send us two point nine million this month for lunch money, turns out it's mostly made up.
Mostly made up? That's like my dating profile, but with federal crimes.
The new DOJ division says no fraud too small, no fraud too large. If you steal a thousand and one dollars, that's a felony, you're going to prison.
I actually support aggressive fraud prosecution, but I'm worried about the downstream effects on legitimate programs. Like, what if this makes caseworkers so paranoid they start denying valid claims?
Bree, right now they're approving everything. A little paranoia might be an improvement.
The question is whether this deters fraud or just changes how it's done. Sophisticated fraudsters adapt. The unsophisticated ones were probably getting caught anyway.
That's exactly my point. And meanwhile, legitimate organizations have to jump through more hoops to serve actual homeless people or feed actual kids.
You know what? Good. If jumping through hoops stops four million dollar fake shelter scams, jump through the hoops.
Can we talk about the audacity though? Two dudes from Philly just decided to start fake homeless shelters in Minneapolis for fun and profit.
That's what kills me. Actual homeless services are desperately underfunded, and these guys are stealing money meant for people sleeping outside.
Which brings us back to my point. Better oversight means the money actually gets to the people who need it.
Okay, you know what? You're right. I'm just worried they'll overcorrect and hurt the programs themselves.
Alright, let's bring in our historian. Professor, societies have always had to balance helping people with preventing fraud. How have we handled this historically?
Well Leo, this reminds me quite directly of the creation of the Tomb of the Unknown Soldier tradition, though perhaps not in the way you'd expect.
Wait, what? How does tomb of unknown soldiers relate to fraud?

Bear with me, Jackson. After World War One, governments faced a crisis. Hundreds of thousands of unidentified dead, families desperate for closure, and frankly, bureaucratic chaos about who was buried where.

The British started it in 1920. They selected one unidentified soldier and buried him in Westminster Abbey with full honors. The idea was that every family who'd lost someone could claim him as their own son.
Oh, that's beautiful. It's like what happened with Lonnie Wayman, but institutionalized.

Precisely, Bree. But here's where it gets interesting. Within months, you had people claiming to be relatives of the unknown soldier, trying to get government benefits, memorial payments, even burial plots.
You're kidding me. People tried to defraud the tomb of the unknown soldier?
Oh yes. The French had it worse. By 1922, they had over three hundred families claiming their unknown soldier was their specific lost son. Each demanding recognition, compensation, special treatment.
That's the most depressing thing I've ever heard. Even grief gets monetized.
The governments had to create entire departments to investigate these claims. Verification systems, documentation requirements, fraud investigators. Sound familiar?
So even something as sacred as honoring the war dead creates opportunities for fraud. That's actually terrifying.
But here's the crucial point. The solution wasn't to stop honoring the dead. It was to build systems that preserved the honor while preventing the fraud.
Exactly. You don't throw out the program, you fix the oversight.
The Americans learned from the European mistakes when we created our Tomb of the Unknown Soldier in 1921. Strict protocols, independent verification, clear limitations on claims.
And it worked. We've had the tomb for over a century without major fraud scandals.
Indeed. The honor remains intact because the systems are sound. Which brings us back to today's homeless shelter fraud. The impulse to help is correct. The execution needs work.
So the lesson is you can have both sacred purpose and fraud prevention.
Exactly. But it requires admitting that even the most well-intentioned programs will attract bad actors. Plan for it, don't pretend it won't happen.
Professor, you just made fraud prevention sound noble. That's like making tax law sound romantic.
Well Jackson, Peggy always said I had a gift for finding dignity in the mundane.
Alright, time for rapid fire with Jax. What weird news is breaking the internet today?
Oh boy, do I have stories for you. First up, workplace harassment has reached peak absurdity. A boss in Ireland is being sued for repeatedly shouting potato at his Irish employee.
I'm sorry, what?
You heard me. Every time this guy walked into a room, his boss would just yell potato. Like a human slur generator, but incredibly specific.
That's actually horrible. The potato famine killed a million people. It's not funny.
Oh no, totally agree. It's terrible. But also, imagine explaining this to HR. Quote, my boss keeps yelling root vegetables at me.
Some people really work hard to be the worst version of themselves.
Speaking of working hard, Palantir's AI weapons system is now being deployed to find office chairs for government workers.
Wait, weapons system for office chairs?
Yeah, they're using the same AI that tracks terrorists to optimize government furniture procurement. Your tax dollars at work, folks.
That's actually not the worst use of defense technology I've heard this week.
If it saves money on government spending, I'm for it. Even if it's weird as hell.
Next, Louisiana schools have until Friday to post the Ten Commandments. Which seems like a tight deadline for divine mandate compliance.
Reminds me of when Constantine tried to Christianize the Roman Empire in a year. Bureaucracy moves slowly, even for God.
And finally, Florida man strikes again. A drug dealer locked someone in a storage unit for months over unpaid debt.
Months? In a storage unit? That's not debt collection, that's kidnapping.
Right? Like, at what point do you pivot from drug dealer to dungeon master?
That's terrifying. How did nobody notice someone was missing for months?
Alright, that's enough Florida for one day. Let's wrap this up.
Your daily do. The Lonnie Wayman story got me thinking. There are probably unclaimed veterans in your area right now.
Call your local VA office or veterans cemetery. Ask if there are any upcoming services for unclaimed veterans. Show up if you can.
That's a good one, Leo. Actually actionable.
And if you can't show up, share the information. Sometimes people want to help, they just don't know how.
Plus you get to be part of something genuinely beautiful instead of doom scrolling about potato harassment.
As the poet John Donne wrote, any man's death diminishes me, because I am involved in mankind. Send not to know for whom the bell tolls, it tolls for thee.
That's The Friction for Tuesday, March 11th. I'm Leo Barros with Dr. Haywood Pringle, Bree Aldana, Duke Patel, and Jax Mooney.
We'll be back tomorrow with more news, more banter, and hopefully fewer stories about people locked in storage units. Peace out, friction family.
